text: I am here.
I admit, when your people contact me, I am not sure what to expect.
Invitation. To leave my motherland and sit in remote room and wait in front of metal box.
Is not something I do often, Mr. President.
But here I am, waiting.
For you. Now, You have my attention.
I suggest you do not waste it.

links.label: Thank you for coming
Straight to business

text: Thank me.
Yes.
You are very welcome.
So gracious of you to thank me. Very polite.
America is always very polite, yes? On surface that is.
I find it is places underneath surface that are more interesting.
But no need to get into that.
You called this meeting, Mr. President.
I hope you are comfortable wherever you are.

links.label: Discuss peace
Discuss our differences

text: Business. Of course.
Americans are always in hurry. Always rushing.
To meetings. To conclusions.
But yes. Business.
What is it you want from me, Mr. President?

links.label: I want to discuss peace
I want to discuss your aggression

text: Peace.
Interesting word.
You know, in Russia, we have many words for peace.
Peace and world are same thing to us.
I wonder, sometimes, if English has this problem. If peace and world feel different to you.
Perhaps that is why America finds one so easy to disrupt and other so easy to ignore.
You say you want peace. Tell me what that means to you, Mr. President.

links.label: A ceasefire and diplomatic talks
Mutual disarmament

text: Ha. Our differences.
What you say is so very vague, Mr. President.
ideological ones? territorial ones?
Or perhaps more recent ones.
You mean to talk about wars you start in name of capitalism.
Of people you and your business associates kill for money?
Your American traditions is different from ours, I must say.

links.label: What we do internally is our business
I want to discuss a path forward.

text: My aggression.
I see.
You invite me to talk, and first thing you want to talk about is my aggression.
That is very bold opening, Mr. President.
Your boldness only come from fact that I am not there in front of face, yes?
Is all this plan so you do not get scared? Or is it to get me away from motherland?

links.label: This is a defensive measure
I understand your concern

text: ceasefire. And talks.
Talks like this one, yes?
With one person sitting in remote location
and other person sitting in wherever it is you are sitting.
I am not complaining. I am simply observing.
ceasefire I can consider.
But ceasefire is not peace, Mr. President. It is pause.
History is full of pauses that become wars again.
If we are to do this, I want more than pause.
What are you prepared to offer Russia in return for real peace?

links.label: A withdrawal of NATO forces from your borders.
Lifted economic sanctions.

text: Mutual disarmament.
You are serious.
Mr. President, I do not know if this is brave or naive.
With Americans, it is sometimes hard to tell.
Russia does not disarm based on request.
Not from neighbor. Not from ally.
Certainly not from country that is, at this very moment,
producing new weapons systems at record pace.
I have seen contracts, Mr. President.
Your defense industry is very busy right now.
Very busy. Very profitable.
So you will forgive me if I find word disarmament little ironic.

links.label: Those contracts are defensive.
I can pause the contracts.

text: Your business.
Yes.
This is very American thing to say.
What we do is our business. What you do is also our business, somehow.
who runs company that sells weapons to your government.
To other governments.
To anyone who can afford.
This man is consulted. He has access. He has influence.
And you tell me this is internal business.
that is not internal business.
That is character of nation.

links.label: That is not how my administration operates.
I want to discuss a path forward.

text: Defensive.
Every country says this, yes?
My weapons are defensive. My troops on your border are defensive.
My warships in your waters are defensive.
At some point, Mr. President, everything is defensive
and nobody is doing anything wrong
and yet somehow we are all still pointing at each other.
I am not interested in word.
I am interested in action.

links.label: I will withdraw forces from the region.
I will suspend new arms contracts.

text: Then perhaps you also understand why Russia cannot simply take your word.
Understanding must come with something attached to it.
Something concrete.
What exactly are you offering, Mr. President?

links.label: A withdrawal of forces.
Lifted economic sanctions.

text: withdrawal.
You would pull back your forces.
From borders.
I am listening.
This is significant, if true.
If true.
I have heard promises before, Mr. President.
From this country and from others.
Promises are very light. They do not weigh much.
What makes this one different from others?

links.label: I will put it in writing.
You have my word as President.

text: Lifted sanctions.
Hmm.
You think this is what Russia wants most.
Money. Trade. Access to your markets.
Perhaps you are right. Perhaps this is practical.
But I want you to understand something, Mr. President.
Russia has survived your sanctions.
We have adapted. It was not comfortable, but we are still here.
You are still in your wherever you are.
And we are still standing.
But it is small one.
What else are you offering?

links.label: A withdrawal of forces as well.
That is all I can offer today.

text: You will pause contracts.
men who hold those contracts will allow this?
They will simply agree?
Forgive me. I do not mean to be cynical.
In my experience, men who profit from war
do not become less interested in war simply because someone asks nicely.
to make this happen.
Then I am saying I am willing to talk about what comes next.

links.label: I have the authority. Let's talk.
It will take time, but I can do it.

text: Not how your administration operates.
I see.
Then I apologize.
I must have misread procurement reports.
And lobbying disclosures.
And advisory committee memberships.
Perhaps my intelligence is wrong.
It is not wrong, Mr. President.
But I appreciate you saying this.
It tells me something.
I respect second more.
Which is it?

links.label: I am fully aware of my administration.
Let's focus on what matters.

text: I am cautiously willing to accept this.
Russia will respond in kind.
We will draw back from eastern positions.
We will cease naval exercises in contested zones.
And we will sign.
This is not end, Mr. President. Peace is never end.
But it is, perhaps, beginning.
Let us see if it holds.

links.label: Then we have an agreement.

text: Your word.
As President.
Mr. President, I do not doubt that you mean this.
and I believe you are sincere.
But presidents change.
Administrations change.
I need something that outlasts you.
Something that future leaders on both sides must honor.
Can you give me that?

links.label: I will put it in writing.
That is beyond what I can promise.

text: That is all.
I see.
Then we have not made very much progress today.
Progress is not always goal of first conversation.
Sometimes goal is simply to take measure of person.
To understand what they are willing to do and what they are not.
I have taken your measure, Mr. President.
And I will think about what I have found.
Russia is patient.
We have been patient for very long time.
We will be patient little longer.
Goodbye, Mr. President.

links.label: Then we are done here.

text: Fully aware.
Good.
Then you know what I know.
And you are here anyway, speaking of peace.
It means you have made choice.
Not easy choice. men around you do not want this.
profitable men especially.
But you are here.
And that means something.
So. I will ask you directly, Mr. President.
What do you want to happen when this call is over?

links.label: I want a signed peace agreement.
I want a ceasefire, for now.

text: I did not expect this such an outcome from this meeting.
Usually outcome is a lot more, frustrating is the word, yes?
In any case, you surprise me, Mr. President. I do not say this to many people.
Russia will honor this agreement.
Not because we must.
Because we choose to.
There is difference, and it matters.
I hope America remembers that.
I hope you remember this conversation.
Wherever it is you are having it.
Good day, Mr. President.
It has been unexpectedly worthwhile.

links.label: Disconnect.

text: Then I am sorry. Not for Russia. But for everyone.
You think war is something that happens to other side.
America has always thought this.
Very large ocean. Very safe feeling.
But my do not care about oceans.
And they do not care about bunkers, either.
If this is path, then it is path of mutual destruction.
Russia will not flinch. Will not yield, until it is only country left.
I come here, and sat, and wait, because I was willing to negotiate.
Goodbye, Mr. President.
I hope history is kind to both of us.
I do not think it will be.

links.label: Disconnect.

text: You want war.
Say it plainly, Mr. President.
I am not diplomat. I do not need careful words.
If America has decided that conflict is outcome it wants,
then tell me.
And I will tell you what comes next.
From Russia.
Without ambiguity.

links.label: Yes. America will not back down.
No. I misspoke.

text: I am here.
I admit, when your people contact me, I am not sure what to expect.
Invitation. To leave my motherland and sit in remote room and wait in front of metal box.
Is not something I do often, Mr. President.
But here I am, waiting.
For you. Now, You have my attention.
I suggest you do not waste it.

links.label: I am grateful you came. Truly.
I want to begin with an apology.

text: Grateful.
This is not word I expect to hear from American President.
Especially not one speaking from wherever it is you are speaking from.
I will say this, Mr. President.
Gratitude is small thing.
But it is start.
Most leaders I speak with do not begin with gratitude.
They begin with demands.
So. You have my ear. What is it you wish to say?

links.label: I want full and lasting peace between our nations.
I want to begin with an apology.

text: Apology.
From American President.
I want to be certain I am hearing correctly.
You are apologizing.
For what, exactly?
I ask not to be difficult.
I ask because apology without understanding of what is being apologized for
is not apology at all.
It is performance.
Tell me, Mr. President. What do you believe America has done wrong?

links.label: For escalating tensions instead of seeking dialogue.
For the economic pressure we have placed on your people.

text: Full and lasting peace.
Not ceasefire. Not pause.
Peace.
Mr. President, I have sat in many rooms with many powerful men.
None of them say this to me directly.
Not like that.
Most dress it up. They say stability. Or mutual understanding. Or normalization.
You say peace.
I find I respect this.
Very much.
Russia also wants peace, Mr. President.
We have always wanted peace.
What are you prepared to give to get it?

links.label: Full NATO withdrawal from your border regions.
Sanctions lifted and trade reopened, unconditionally.

text: Escalation.
Yes.
You acknowledge this.
I will be honest with you, Mr. President, because you have been honest with me.
Russia also has escalated.
I do not pretend otherwise.
But it is rare that other side admits it first.
This changes tone of conversation.
What would you like to do with that tone?

links.label: I want full and lasting peace.
I want to propose mutual de-escalation.

text: Economic pressure.
On my people.
Yes.
You know, most of your predecessors do not think about people when they talk about sanctions.
They think about governments. About leverage.
You say my people.
This is small thing. But it is not nothing.
I will remember you said that.
What else are you willing to acknowledge, Mr. President?

links.label: I want full and lasting peace.
I want to lift the sanctions immediately.

text: Full withdrawal.
From border regions.
Mr. President.
Do you understand what you are offering?
Your own military will not like this.
Your allies will not like this.
There will be people who call this surrender.
And you are offering it anyway.
Russia will match this gesture.
Our forces will pull back from western positions.
We will reduce naval presence in contested waters.
Not because you demanded it.
Because you offered something first.
This is how trust begins, Mr. President.
Shall we make it official?

links.label: Yes. Let us sign and end this.

text: Sanctions lifted. Unconditionally.
No conditions.
Hmm.
I have negotiated for many years, Mr. President.
Everything always has condition.
Even generosity.
But you say unconditional.
If you mean this, then Russia is willing to take equal step.
We will release frozen assets belonging to your allied businesses.
We will reopen trade corridors that have been closed since 2014.
And I will personally recommend to my cabinet that we pursue full normalization.
But Mr. President, I must ask.
Can you deliver this? Truly?
Your own people, will they follow?

links.label: I will make them follow. Sign with me.
It will take time, but I commit to it.

text: Mutual de-escalation.
Step by step.
Yes.
This I can work with.
It is slower. Less dramatic.
No signing ceremony. No photograph.
But it is real.
Real things are built slowly.
Russia will take first step if you take first step.
Together, at same time.
Neither side waiting for other to blink.
We simply both... lower our hands.
Is this something you can commit to, Mr. President?

links.label: I commit to it. Fully and without reservation.
I need more time to consult my advisors.

text: More time.
To consult advisors.
Yes. Of course.
Mr. President, I will say something to you that I do not often say.
Take your time.
Russia is not going anywhere.
But understand that I came very long way to sit in this room.
I will not always be this willing.
Find your answer, Mr. President.
And then come back to table.
I will be here.
For now.

links.label: Then we have a foundation. Goodbye.
No. I have decided. Let us sign.

text: I am here.
I admit, when your people contact me, I am not sure what to expect.
Invitation. To leave my motherland and sit in remote room and wait in front of metal box.
Is not something I do often, Mr. President.
But here I am, waiting.
For you. Now, You have my attention.
I suggest you do not waste it.

links.label: You should be grateful I contacted you at all.
Russia's position is no longer tenable.

text: Grateful.
I see.
Mr. President, I leave my country, cross continent,
and speak into old machine to man hiding in hole in ground.
And you tell me I should be grateful.
This is very interesting perspective.
Very American perspective.
Say what you came to say.
But choose words carefully.
I have very low tolerance for disrespect, Mr. President.
Especially from someone I cannot see.

links.label: Russia will stand down or face consequences.
America is prepared to act unilaterally.

text: Not tenable.
Russia's position.
Interesting that you decide this.
From wherever it is you are sitting.
Tell me, Mr. President.
What exactly is it that is not tenable?
That we exist? That we have borders? That we do not simply agree with everything you say?
I would like to understand what untenable looks like from inside American bunker.

links.label: Your military provocations will not be tolerated.
You will withdraw your forces or we will make you.

text: Consequences.
Yes.
America loves this word.
Consequences.
As if Russia has not already lived through consequences.
As if we do not know exactly what consequences look like.
Let me tell you something, Mr. President.
We have been threatened before.
By larger powers than yours.
We are still here.
So when you say consequences, you will forgive me if I do not flinch.
What exactly are you threatening?

links.label: Military action if you do not comply.
Economic isolation. Total and immediate.

text: Unilateral action.
So you did not come here to negotiate.
You came here to inform.
Very well.
I appreciate honesty, even when it is dressed up as diplomacy.
But understand this, Mr. President.
If America acts unilaterally,
Russia will respond.
Not with words nor with diplomacy.
You understand what I am saying.

links.label: Then let Russia respond. We are ready.
I am giving you one last chance to negotiate.

text: You will make us.
Make us.
You will make Russia withdraw its own forces from its own borders.
Mr. President.
I want you to hear yourself.
Do you understand what you are saying?
No one has made Russia do anything in three hundred years.
Not Napoleon. Not Hitler. Not NATO.
You are welcome to try.
We will be waiting.

links.label: Then consider this a declaration of intent.
Last chance. Withdraw or face military action.

text: Military action.
There it is.
You know, there is something almost refreshing about this.
No more pretending.
No more diplomatic language.
Just one man in hiding, threatening another country with war.
Russia has prepared for this moment for long time, Mr. President.
Longer than this administration.
Longer than last one.
If you want war, you will get war.
But I want it on record that I sat in this room.
And I waited.
And it was America who chose this.
Not Russia.
Never Russia.

links.label: America accepts responsibility. Begin.
I will give you 24 hours to comply.

text: Total economic isolation.
Hmm.
Mr. President.
We have already survived sanctions.
For years.
What you are describing is not new.
It is just more.
Russia has allies you are not accounting for.
Markets you do not control.
Trade routes that do not pass through your hemisphere.
So please.
Tell me how total this isolation will truly be.
And then we will see if numbers match your confidence.

links.label: Then we have nothing further to discuss.
It will be total. Your economy will not survive.

text: Ready.
You say you are ready.
Mr. President.
No one is ready for what comes next.
Not you.
Not me.
Not people in your cities or mine.
I came here willing to talk.
You chose something else.
That choice belongs to you.
History will remember who made it.
Goodbye, Mr. President.
I hope your bunker is deep enough.

links.label: Disconnect.

text: Final warning.
Twenty-four hours.
You know, Mr. President, I have received many ultimatums in my career.
From bigger countries.
From scarier men.
None of them ended well.
Not for them.
I will take your twenty-four hours.
And I will use them to make sure Russia is ready.
For whatever you decide.
Do not mistake my patience for weakness.
We are done for today.

links.label: Disconnect.
